title: Sorting
description: Rust provides sort(), sort_by(), and sort_by_key() on Vec — all in-place and O(n log n). Master these to handle custom comparisons, multi-key sorting, and reverse ordering that interview problems demand.

sections.title: sort() and reverse
sections.explanation: `.sort()` sorts a Vec in-place in ascending order (requires elements to implement Ord). `.reverse()` reverses in-place. For descending sort, chain `.sort()` then `.reverse()`, or use `.sort_by(|a, b| b.cmp(a))`. Note: `.sort()` is stable (preserves order of equal elements).

exercise.prompt: Sort the vector [5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3] in ascending order and print it. Then reverse it for descending order and print again.

validation.message: Use .sort() for ascending and .reverse() to flip to descending

exercise.hint: Call .reverse() after .sort() to get descending order.

sections.title: sort_by() with custom comparison
sections.explanation: `.sort_by(|a, b| a.cmp(b))` takes a closure returning `Ordering`. Use `b.cmp(a)` for descending. For **f64** (which doesn't implement Ord), use `.sort_by(|a, b| a.partial_cmp(b).unwrap())`. This is the most flexible sorting method.

exercise.prompt: Sort the vector [4, 2, 7, 1, 9] in descending order using sort_by with b.cmp(a). Then sort floats [3.5, 1.2, 4.8, 2.1] in ascending order using partial_cmp. Print both.

validation.message: Use b.cmp(a) for descending and partial_cmp for float comparison

exercise.hint: b.cmp(a) reverses the comparison for descending order.

sections.title: sort_by_key() for derived sorting
sections.explanation: `.sort_by_key(|x| key)` sorts by a derived key rather than the element itself. This is perfect for sorting by string length, absolute value, or a specific struct field. For descending, wrap the key in `std::cmp::Reverse`.

exercise.prompt: Sort strings ["rust", "go", "python", "c"] by length using sort_by_key. Then sort numbers [5, -3, 1, -8, 2] by absolute value. Print both results.

validation.message: Sorted by length: c, go, rust, python. Sorted by abs: 1, 2, -3, 5, -8
Use sort_by_key with .len() for strings and .abs() for numbers

exercise.hint: Use w.len() as the key for string length sorting.

sections.title: Sorting tuples and multi-key sort
sections.explanation: Tuples in Rust compare lexicographically, so `Vec<(i32, i32)>` sorts by first element, then second. This gives you multi-key sorting for free. For custom orders on different keys, use `sort_by` with a closure that compares each field explicitly.

exercise.prompt: Sort the intervals [(3,6), (1,4), (2,5), (1,2)] by default tuple ordering and print. Then re-sort by end time using sort_by_key and print again.

validation.message: Default sort: by start then end. Sort by end: same result for this input.
Use .sort() for tuple ordering and sort_by_key with the end field

exercise.hint: Use `end` as the key in the sort_by_key closure to sort by end time.
